name: Korean Fried Chicken (Dakgangjeong)
why: You've been exploring Korean cuisine — this is the iconic crispy, sticky, gochujang-glazed chicken that defines Korean street food. The cornflour coating gives an impossibly crunchy texture, and the sweet-spicy sauce is addictive. A proper step up from takeaway.
author: Henry
ingredients: 1 large chunk ginger, peeled and finely grated
Sesame seeds and sliced spring onion, to serve
Small piece of ginger, peeled and grated

steps: Put the dark brown sugar, gochujang, soy sauce, crushed garlic, grated ginger, and sesame oil in a small saucepan.
Simmer over medium heat for 3-4 minutes until the mixture thickens to a syrupy glaze.
Remove from heat and set aside.
Season the chicken wings generously with salt, pepper, and the grated ginger.
Place the cornflour in a large bowl or zip-lock bag.
Toss the chicken wings in the cornflour until completely and evenly coated — shake off any excess.
Heat about 2cm of vegetable oil in a large frying pan over medium-high heat (around 170°C/340°F).
Carefully add the coated chicken wings in a single layer — don't overcrowd the pan.
Fry for 8-10 minutes until golden brown and crisp, turning halfway through.
Remove from the oil and drain on kitchen paper. Let cool slightly for about 2 minutes.
Reheat the sauce gently in the saucepan.
Toss the crispy chicken wings in the warm sauce until evenly coated and sticky.
Transfer to a serving bowl and scatter over sesame seeds and sliced spring onions.
Serve immediately while still crispy.

pro_tips: For extra crunch, double-fry the chicken: fry once at 160°C, rest for 5 minutes, then fry again at 180°C for 2 minutes before glazing.
Swap chicken wings for drumsticks or thigh pieces if you prefer meatier bites — just extend frying time by 3-4 minutes.

name: Vietnamese-Style Caramel Pork
why: A proper step into Vietnamese cooking — sweet, sticky, and spicy pork stir-fried in a caramelised fish sauce glaze. It's a 30-minute weeknight dinner that tastes like it came from a street food stall, with a completely different flavour profile to anything you've cooked recently.
author: Henry
ingredients: 500g pork shoulder steaks, cut into bite-sized chunks
1 red chilli, deseeded and finely chopped
1 tbsp fish sauce (plus extra to taste)
4 spring onions, halved and shredded lengthways

steps: Cut the pork shoulder into bite-sized chunks (about 2-3cm pieces).
Finely chop the shallots.
Grate the ginger — use a microplane or the fine side of a grater.
Deseed and finely chop the red chilli.
Shred the spring onions lengthways and set aside for garnish.
If using fresh pak choi, separate the leaves and roughly chop.
Heat 1 tbsp of the groundnut oil in a wok or large frying pan over high heat.
Add the pork in a single layer — don't overcrowd, work in batches if needed.
Stir-fry for 3-4 minutes until the pork is well-browned all over. The high heat is key for a good sear.
Remove the pork with a slotted spoon and set aside on a plate.
Turn the heat down to low. Add the remaining ½ tbsp oil to the wok.
Stir in the shallots, ginger, and chilli. Cook gently for 2 minutes until just starting to soften — you want the aromatics to release their flavour without burning.
Add the brown sugar, fish sauce, and 200ml water. Stir everything together until the sugar dissolves.
Bring to a boil, stirring constantly.
Return the browned pork to the pan.
Increase heat and bubble vigorously for 8-10 minutes. The sauce will reduce and thicken, turning glossy and sticky as it coats the meat. Keep stirring occasionally.
Taste the sauce — add a little more fish sauce if it needs extra saltiness, and stir in the chilli sauce for heat.
Scatter the shredded spring onions over the top.
Serve immediately with steamed rice and blanched pak choi.
The rice soaks up the caramel sauce — don't skimp on it.

pro_tips: The sugar-to-fish sauce ratio is the heart of this dish. If you like it sweeter, add an extra tablespoon of brown sugar. If you prefer more punch, add another splash of fish sauce. Taste and adjust — the sauce should be sweet-first with a salty umami finish.

Don't skip browning the pork first — the Maillard reaction on the pork surface gives it a deep, savoury flavour that carries through the sauce. If the pan is too small, cook in two batches rather than steaming everything at once.
Pak choi is optional if you can't find it — baby spinach, bok choy, or even just extra rice work fine. The dish is brilliant on its own.
